titleStmt.title: Introduction to the site of Mount Tao

div.head: 陶山
div.p: 陶山和龍山是山東佛教摩崖刻經最南端的兩座山。陶山的銘刻僅於1997年引起學術界的關注。除了一個無法辨認的經主名外，只有兩佛名及“般若波羅蜜”之稱名可辨識。然而這三個稱名的組合，讓我們對於僧人對“空觀”的理解及其佛教宇宙的理念，得出影響深遠的結論。

figure.head: Mount Tao seen from the south; photograph taken in 2007
陶山南面全景。2007年拍攝

div.head: 地理位置
div.p: 陶山隸屬滕州市羊莊鎮，位於滕州市東南方23公里，棗莊市東北偏北15公里。該山座落於一排串珠狀山頭的最北端。山體呈山頂平坦的橢圓錐體，南北長0.7公里左右。其山峰高於四周平地約100米，海拔高度170米左右。薛河蜿蜒環繞山體成半圓，再繼續流向西南方。陶山地理位置是東經117.3321度，北緯34.9324度，相當於UTM坐標系統第50S帶，東行530330，北行386500。
我們調查這個刻經址時，偶然發現了一些具有五千年以上歷史的大汶口文化的陶瓷碎片。罕見近期人跡建築，唯有西邊山脚建了一些屬於西陶山村的房子。
附近刻有佛教銘刻的龍山位於西南方5.7公里處。從陶山可看到龍山，但無法見到其刻經址，因它位於山峰的另一邊。鄒城周邊最南方的刻經山嶧山，距離陶山西北偏北方51.7公里。

div.head: 地形與空間構成
div.p: 陶山平坦的山頂，有一塊約20×40米見方的光滑石坪，該石坪自南至北逐漸往下傾斜。其石灰岩層的山頂風化成梯形狀。銘文刊刻於石坪北面略微偏東處，朝向東北偏北。經主名號刻於右側，即佛名之西。

figure.head: Map of the summit of Mount Tao
陶山山頂等高綫圖
Mount Tao, northern end of the summit’s plateau with the inscriptions in the foreground; photograph taken in 2007
陶山山頂石坪的北端及刻銘。2007年拍攝

div.head: 地名
div.p: 陶山，又名華彩山。安廷山 第134頁。

div.head: 相關寺院
div.p: 柱孔遍布於梯狀石灰岩山頂，大部分爲圓孔。直徑最長43釐米，最短8釐米，中等尺寸爲16釐米，其中有兩個大約19×44釐米的矩形孔眼（ ）。這些都可證明此處曾有建築物。至今没有找到關於其年代的信息。

div.head: 相關人物
div.p: 安廷山提出，經主名可釋讀爲“安公”，並藉此將陶山的銘刻與山東刻經址的關鍵人物即僧人書法家“僧安道壹”（活動於562－579 年）聯繫起來。 安廷山 第135頁。除此，未見他説。

div.head: 斷代
div.p: 刻銘無紀年，不過其書法風格近似山東省其他刻銘，例如洪頂山（洪頂山9.10號和洪頂山16.2號；550－564年）、《中國佛教石經•山東省》第一卷，第191－194頁和第255－261頁。徂徠山（徂徠山2號和徂徠山9號；570年）見本卷徂徠山“刻銘”。及鐵山（鐵山1號；579年）。《中國佛教石經•山東省》第二卷，第117－147頁。

div.head: 研究史
div.p: 陶山的銘刻不爲清代金石學家所知。安廷山於1997年發表的《山東摩崖書刻藝術》首次深入探討， 安廷山。該書主要關注佛教與道教的摩崖書刻藝術。後來對此刻經址的調查，未增加新資料。

div.head: 總目
div.p: 陶山刊刻二佛名及一“般若波羅蜜”稱名（陶山1號），此外還有一則題記（陶山2號）。

div.head: 陶山1號
div.p: 以三名爲一組直行書寫，自右至左並排刊刻阿弥陁佛、觀世音佛及般若波羅蜜。這兩個亦刊刻於洪頂山和徂徠山的佛名，確立了觀世音佛爲現在西方净土世界阿彌陀佛的直接繼任者的身份。

如本卷上文所示，見徂徠山“刻銘”部分，。 觀世音佛（和大勢至佛）繼承阿彌陀佛的説法出自三部經典，即《悲華經》的兩個漢譯本《悲華經》T#157，3：167－233c8。《大乘悲分陀利經》T#158，3：233c13－289a。 和《觀世音菩薩授記經》。《觀世音菩薩授記經》T#371，12：353b5－357c17。 此外，至少還有一部未入藏的經典描述觀世音成佛的事迹，即《觀世音十大願經》，又名《弘猛慧海經》。“《觀世音十大願經》一卷。仁壽録云：一名‘大悲觀世音經’，具題云‘大悲觀世音弘猛慧海十大願品第七百’。”《開元釋教録》T#2154，55：675b12－13。首次提及此經的《歴代三寶紀》稱該經屬於“大乘修多羅， 失譯”，《歴代三寶紀》T#2034，49：113c5。隋代及後來的藏經目録均標示此經爲疑僞經。《衆經目録》T#2146，55：126b22－23，法經（生卒年不詳）撰。《衆經目録》T#2147，55：172c13－14， 彦琮（活動於七世紀中）於602年完成。《大周刊定眾經目録》T#2153，55：472c8－9，明佺（卒於712年之後）撰。《開元釋教録》T#2154，55：675b12－13。經文描述名爲善光太子者，得空王觀世音佛授記於未來成佛，發願於未來行菩薩道時名觀世音。這十大願中的兩項明顯涉及波若，即“乘波若船”和“得智慧風”。

《觀世音十大願經》這部疑僞經的佚文僅存於佛典的抄引節文慧沼（650－714）的《十一面神咒心經義疏》T#1802, 39：1004b－1011c13是玄奘（約602－664）譯《十一面神呪心經》T#1071, 20: 152–55之註解本，該義疏引用了十大願。十大願引文的開頭：“弘猛海慧經曰：昔此閻浮提有王名曰善首，有五百王子，第一太子名善光。値空王觀世音佛乃發十願。”T#1802，39：1006c27－1007a1。吉藏（549－623）所撰的四部關於《法華經》的註疏，其中最早的一部《法華玄論》T#1720，34：361a－450c指出“弘猛海惠經明，觀音過去世値空王觀世音佛發願，願未來名觀世音。” T#1720，34：449a29－449b1。以及兩處石刻。河北涉縣木井寺一石碑上所刻《遺教經》本包含觀世音十大願。該碑紀年573年，十大願刊刻於碑側，題爲“大悲觀世音願”。河北曲陽縣八會寺南側佛龕東面的弘猛海慧經觀音願之下，也有刊刻於隋朝的十大願（題記紀年593年）。馬忠理第43頁。倉本尚德第229、232頁。倉本尚德第11－12頁。倉本尚德第464、467頁。氣賀澤保規。趙洲第22頁。倉本尚德第228、232頁。倉本尚德第460、467－468頁。徂徠山及陶山摩崖石刻刊刻時，此經已流通。因此它可能是理解觀世音佛和在陶山刊刻於其側的般若波羅蜜二者關係的關鍵。
般若波羅蜜是指與成佛有關的智慧，是菩薩道上修行六波羅蜜的第六法，且經常等同於對“空性”的理解。般若波羅蜜亦被擬人化，刊刻於另外兩個佛名之側。它是智慧的召唤，而此智慧又稱佛母，是成就圓覺不可缺少的要件。

山東另外三個刻經址的佛名緊鄰般若經節文。洪頂山的佛名群組（洪頂山9.1－9.15號；洪頂山10－13號）刊刻於《文殊師利所説般若波羅蜜經》《文殊師利所説摩訶般若波羅蜜經》T#232，8：731a15－21。《文殊師利所説般若波羅蜜經》T#233，8：738a19－24。洪頂山16.2號。九十八字節文之下及側面，該經叙述佛陀爲文殊師利闡明般若波羅蜜。關於這段節文，見《中國佛教石經•山東省》第一卷，第65頁和第255－261頁。徂徠山光化寺附近有一刊刻節録自《摩訶般若波羅蜜經》《摩訶般若波羅蜜經》T#223，8：250b3－7。十七空節文的巨石，據説現已不存的佛名（徂徠山9號和徂徠山10號）原刊刻於此巨石附近，或直接刻於此巨石之上 。在這些刻經址，大空王佛刻銘始終緊鄰闡述般若波羅蜜及其相同的概念──空（洪頂山8號和徂徠山10號）的佛經節文。尖山也是如此，其九十八字節文（尖山6號）刻於大空王佛名（尖山10號）之上。見。如此排列，更加强化了佛名與“般若波羅蜜”或“空”這個教法的密切聯繫。
陶山的佛名排序與原本刊刻於徂徠山巨石的佛名順序相近。徂徠山巨石“大空王佛”（徂徠山10號）在“觀世音佛”（徂徠山9號）之後。而陶山“觀世音佛”佛名之後接續的是“般若波羅蜜”，顯示此名與其他刻經址著名的“大空王佛”地位相等。

div.head: 陶山2號
div.p: 這則題記只有開頭的“經主”二字清晰可辨。由此或可推測，除佛名外，此處曾另刻一段佛經節文，或至少曾有刊刻計劃。賴非第116頁。洪頂山“大空王佛”（洪頂山21.1號）刻銘的題記可供比較。該題記爲小字“經主釋法洪供□”（洪頂山21.2號）。《中國佛教石經•山東省》第一卷，第284－292頁。佛名與題記似乎自成一組，但事實上，這則題記涉及刊刻於其右側的佛經節文（洪頂山22號）。《中國佛教石經•山東省》第一卷，第71頁，圖8。根據這個比較，也許可提出陶山亦曾刻有佛經節文的假設。
陶山的經主名風化嚴重，無法確認上文所及安廷山的釋讀。 安廷山 第135頁。

div.head: 刊刻技法
div.p: 陶山刻經技法爲“V”字型刻法，即從筆畫兩邊綫斜入鏨，雙邊綫相交，自然形成。

div.head: Mount Tao
div.p: Mount Tao 陶山 and Mount Long 龍山 are the southernmost mountains in Shandong with engraved Buddhist inscriptions. Mount Tao’s inscriptions were brought to the attention of the scholarly world only in 1997. In addition to the unidentifiable name of a donor, only two Buddha names and an invocation of the Perfection of Wisdomhave been recognized. Yet the combination of these three invocations allows far-reaching conclusions about the monks’ understanding of the concept of Emptiness and their ideas about the Buddhist cosmos.

div.head: Location
div.p: Mount Tao is within the Yangzhuang Township 羊莊鎮, under the city of Tengzhou 滕州市. It is located 23 km southeast of Tengzhou City and 15 km to the north-northeast of Zaozhuang City 棗莊市. The mountain is the northernmost peak in a row of outcrops that follow each other like beads on a string. It is shaped like an evenly truncated elliptical cone with a north-south extension of about 0.7 km. Its peak, rising about 100 m above the mostly flat surrounding area, is ca. 170 m above sea level. The River Xue 薛河 winds its way around the mountain in a semicircle and continues in a southwestern direction. Mount Tao’s geographic location is 117.3321° east and 34.9324° north WGS84. This equals 530330 E (easting) and 386500 N (northing) on the meridian 50S UTM.

During our survey of the inscription site, we came across some ceramic sherds of the Dawenkou culture 大汶口文化, more than five thousand years in age. There has been little human activity in recent times. Houses in West Mount Tao Village 西陶山村 have been built at the western foot of the mountain.

The neighboring mountain with a Buddhist inscription, Mount Long, is 5.7 km to the southwest. It is visible from Mount Tao, but its inscription site is not, as it lies beyond the peak. The southernmost of the inscribed mountains around Zoucheng, Mount Yi 嶧山, is at a distance of 51.7 km to the north-northwest of Mount Tao.

div.head: Topography and Spatial Grouping
div.p: On the flat mountain summit, a plateau of smooth rock, ca. 20 x 40 meters, slopes gently down from south to north. The layers of the limestone sediment have weathered into steps. The inscriptions are engraved on the plateau at the northeast. They face north-northeast. A reference to a donor is on the right, that is, to the west of the Buddha names.

div.head: Place Name
div.p: Mount Tao is also called Mount Huacai 華彩. 134.

figure.head: Looking southwest from the summit’s plateau; photograph taken in 2007
由陶山山頂石坪眺望西南方。2007年拍攝

div.head: Related monasteries
div.p: Post holes are distributed over the entire summit on the stepped limestone. Most are circular. The largest diameter is 43 cm, the smallest 8 cm, the mean is 16 cm, and there are two rectangular holes of ca. 19 x 44 cm (fig. ). All these are evidence that buildings once stood here. As yet, no information on their age is available.

div.head: Related Persons
div.p: An Tingshan 安廷山 has suggested that the name of the sutra donor could be read as “Sire An” (Angong 安公). This identification enabled him to connect the carvings at Mount Tao with the key figure of the Shandong sites, the monk calligrapher Seng’an Daoyi 僧安道壹 (fl. 562–579). 135. Apart from this suggestion, no other proposals have been made.

figure.head: Looking southeast from the summit’s plateau; photograph taken in 2007
由陶山山頂石坪眺望東南方。2007年拍攝

div.head: Dating
div.p: The inscriptions are undated. However, the calligraphic style is close to that of other Shandong inscriptions, such as those on Mount Hongding 洪頂山 (HDS 9.10, HDS 16.2; dated 550–564), 191–194, 255–261. Mount Culai 徂徠山 (CLS 2, CLS 9; dated 570),See the present volume, Mount Culai, Inscriptions. and Mount Tie 鐵山 (Tie 1; dated 579). 117–147.

div.head: Research History
div.p: The inscriptions at Mount Tao were not known to the epigraphers of the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. The first thorough study was undertaken by An Tingshan 安廷山 in his 1997 publication on The Art of Calligraphic Engravings on Polished Cliffs in Shandong, . a work that is primarily concerned with the calligraphy of the Buddhist and Daoist polished cliff inscriptions. Later surveys of the site have not added substantially to this information.

div.head: Inventory
div.p: At Mount Tao two Buddha names and one invocation of the Perfection of Wisdom (TS 1) were carved, and, in addition, a colophon (TS 2).

div.head: TS 1
div.p: Three vertically written, parallel engravings are aligned from right to left, Buddha Amitābha (Amituo fo 阿弥陀佛), Buddha Perceiver of the World’s Sounds (Guanshiyin fo 觀世音佛), and Perfection of Wisdom ( prajñāpāramitā , bore boluomi 般若波羅蜜). The two Buddha names, which are also carved at Mount Hongding and Mount Culai, establish the position of the Buddha Perceiver of the World’s Sounds as the immediate successor of Buddha Amitābha in the latter’s Pure Land in the West.

As shown earlier in this volume,See the Introduction to Mount Culai, Inscriptions, pp. . the succession of Buddha Perceiver of the World’s Sounds (and the Buddha Great Power Obtained; Mahā­sthāma­prāpta; Dashizhi 大勢至佛) to Amitābha is based on three canonical sutras, namely two Chinese translations of the Sutra of the Compassionate Lotus Flower (T#157 and T#158), and the Sutra of Bodhisattva Perceiver of the World’s Sounds Receiving a Prophecy (T#371). In addition, at least one non-canonical scripture narrates the circumstances of the Bodhisattva Perceiver of the World’s Sounds becoming a Buddha. This is the Sutra on the Ten Great Vows taken by the Perceiver of the World’s Sounds (Guanshiyin shi da yuan jing 觀世音十大願經), which is also known under the title, Sutra on the Vast and Fierce Ocean-like Wisdom (Hong­meng huihai jing 弘猛慧海經).See the Kaiyuan Era Catalog of Śākyamuni’s Teachings (Kaiyuan shijiao lu
開元釋教録): “Sutra on the Ten Great Vows taken by the Perceiver of the World’s Sounds, in one scroll. The Catalog of the Renshou Era [T#2147, completed in 602 by Yancong 彥琮 (557–610) and others] says: Another name for this sutra is Sutra on the Great Compassionate Perceiver of the World’s Sounds. The full title is Sutra on Ten Great Vows of the Vast and Fierce Ocean-like Wisdom Taken by the Great Compassionate Perceiver of the World’s Sounds, in 700 chapters. 《觀世音十大願經》一卷。 仁壽録云：“‘一名大悲觀世音經’，具題云：大悲觀世音弘猛慧海十大願品第七百。” (T#2154, 55: 675b12–13). This text is first mentioned as a “Mahayana Sutra; translator’s [name] lost”;See the Record of the Three Jewels in Successive Dynasties (Lidai sanbao ji
歷代三寶紀), T#2034, 49: 113c5: “大乘修多羅失譯。” Sui dynasty and later catalogs label it as apocryphal (yiwei 疑僞).See the Catalog of All Canonical Scriptures (Zhongjing mulu 眾經目録) by Fajing 法經 (dates unknown) at T#2146, 55: 126b22–23; the Catalog of All Canonical Scriptures (Zhongjing mulu
眾經目録), completed in 602 by Yancong 彥琮 (mid-seventh century) at T#2147, 55: 172c13–14); and the Catalog of All Canonical Scriptures, Authorized by the Great Zhou (Da Zhou kanding zhongjing mulu 大周刊定眾經目録) by Mingquan 明佺 (d. after 712) at T#2153, 55: 472c8–9, as well as the above mentioned Kaiyuan Era Catalog of Śākyamuni’s Teachings (Kaiyuan shijiao lu 開元釋教録) at T#2154, 55: 675b12–13.

The text narrates, how a certain Prince Shan­guang 善光 received the prophecy about his future Buddhahood from a Buddha called “Buddha King of Emptiness Perceiver of the World’s Sounds 空王觀世音佛”, and thereupon vowed to be called “Perceiver of the World’s Sounds” in a future life, when following the bodhisattva path. Two of the ten vows that he took are explicitly concerned with wisdom ( prajñā ; bore 波若), namely to “sail the boat of wisdom” (cheng bore chuan 乘波若船) and to “catch the wind of wisdom” (de zhihui feng 得智慧風).

The apocryphal Sutra on the Ten Great Vows taken by the Perceiver of the World’s Sounds has only survived in quotations in other works of the Buddhist Canon,The ten vows are quoted in The Meanings of the Heart of the Incantation of the Eleven-Faced One (Shiyi mian shenzhou xin jing yishu 十一面神咒心經義疏), which is a commentary by Huizhao 慧沼 (650–714) on Xuanzang’s 玄奘 (ca. 602–664) translation of the Sutra on the Heart of the Incantation of the Eleven-Faced One (Shiyi mianshenzhou xin jing 十一面神咒心經; T#1071, 20: 152–55). The beginning of the quote on the ten vows reads: “The Sutra on the Vast and Fierce Ocean-like Wisdom says: ‘Once, a king, by the name of Shanshou 善首, lived in this Jambudvīpa, who had five hundred sons. The first-born son, by the name of Shanguang 善光, met Buddha King of Emptiness Perceiver of the World’s Sounds, and, thereupon, took ten vows…弘猛海慧經曰∶昔此閻浮提有王名曰善首，有五百王子，第一太子名善光。值空王觀世音佛乃發十願。’” (T#1802, 39: 1006c27–1007a1). In his first of four commentaries on the Lotus Sutra, The Treatise on the Profundity of the [Sutra on the] Lotus [of the Wonderful] Law (Fahua xuanlun
法華玄論; T#1720, 34: 361a–450c), Jizang 吉藏 (549–623) also points out that “the Sutra on the Vast and Fierce Ocean-like Wisdom explains that, in the past, Perceiver of the [World’s] Sounds met Buddha King of Emptiness Perceiver of the World’s Sounds and made a vow. He vowed to be called ‘Perceiver of the World’s Sounds’ in a future life.弘猛海惠經明，觀音過去世值空王觀世音佛發願，願未來名觀世音。” (T#1720, 34: 449a29–449b1). and in two stone-cut versions. 43, 229, 232, 11–12, and 464, 467 say that the version of the Sutra of the Teaching Left Behind [by the Buddha] (Yijiao jing 遺教經), carved on a stele at Mujing Monastery 木井寺 in She County 涉縣, Hebei, contains the wording of these ten great vows of Perceiver of the World’s Sounds. This stele is dated to the year 573, and the text of the ten vows is found on its narrow right side, under the title “Vows of the Great Compassionate Perceiver of the World’s Sounds” (“Da bei Guanshiyin yuan” 大悲觀世音願). The ten great vows were also carved during the Sui dynasty at Bahui Monastery 八會寺 in Quyang County 曲陽縣, Hebei (colophon dated 593). Here, they are found on the east side of the southern niche, under the heading, Vows taken by the Perceiver of the [World’s] Sounds from the Sutra on the Vast and Fierce Ocean-like Wisdom (Hongmeng huihai jing Guanyin yuan 弘猛慧海經觀音願); see ; 22; 228, 232; 460, 467–468. The sutra was already in circulation when the cliff inscriptions at Mount Culai and Mount Tao were carved. It may thus hold the key for understanding the connection between Buddha Perceiver of the World’s Sounds and the Perfection of Wisdom ( prajñāpāramitā , bore boluomi 般若波羅蜜), carved next to it on Mount Tao.

The Perfection of Wisdom is the wisdom associated with the realization of Buddhahood. It is the sixth of the Six Perfections practiced on the bodhisattva path, and often equated with the knowledge of Emptiness. The Perfection of Wisdom was also personified as its characters are carved alongside the other two Buddha names. It is an evocation of the quality of wisdom, which is said to be the mother of all Buddhas, indispensable for their complete awakening.

figure.head: Rectangular post-holes on the summit’s plateau; photograph taken in 2007
陶山山頂石坪的矩形柱洞。2007年拍攝

div.p:
At three other Shandong inscription sites, Buddha names are placed close to excerpts from Prajñāpāramitā Sutras. At Mount Hongding, groups of Buddha names (HDS 9.1–9.15; HDS 10–13) are located below and next to the passage of 98 characters taken from the Sutra on the Great Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī (Wenshushili suo shuo boreboluomi jing
文殊師利所説般若波羅蜜經 T#232, 8: 731a15–21; T#233, 8: 738a19–24; HDS 16.2), in which the Buddha explains prajñāpāramitā to Mañjuśrī.For this sutra passage, see 90–91 and 255–261. At Mount Culai, the now lost Buddha names (CLS 9 and CLS 10) are said to have been placed close to or even on the boulder near Guang­hua Monastery that is engraved with a passage on the Seventeen Kinds of Emptiness taken from the Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra ( Pañca­viṃśatisāhasrikā­prajñā­pāramitā ; Mohe bore boluomi jing 摩訶般若波羅蜜經; T#223, 8: 250b3–7). At these sites, the name Buddha King of Great Emptiness (Da kong wang fo 大空王佛) is carved close to sutra passages that expound on the Perfection of Wisdom and its twin concept, Emptiness (HDS 8 and CLS 10). The same is true for Mount Jian, where the passage of 98 characters (JS 6) was once located above the name Buddha King of Great Emptiness (JS 10).See the present volume, Mount Jian, fig. . This juxtaposition further strengthens the intimate ties between Buddha names and the teachings of the Perfection of Wisdom, or Emptiness.

The sequence of the names carved here at Mount Tao is closely akin to that originally carved on the boulder at Mount Culai. There, the name, Buddha King of Great Emptiness (CLS 10), follows the name, Buddha Perceiver of the World’s Sounds (CLS 9). At Mount Tao, instead the evocation of the Perfection of Wisdom follows the name, Buddha Perceiver of the World’s Sounds, which indicates that the Buddha King of Great Emptiness celebrated at other sites is equivalent to the Perfection of Wisdom.

div.head: TS 2
div.p: Only the first two characters of the colophon, reading “Sutra Donor” are clearly legible. This title has given rise to the assumption that, originally, in addition to the Buddha names, a sutra passage was also carved, or at least envisioned. 116. The colophon to the inscription “Buddha King of Great Emptiness” at Mount Hongding (HDS 21.1), is comparable. In small characters, it reads “Offered [respectfully] by the Donor of Sutras, Venerable Fahong” (HDS 21.2).See 284–292. Although the Buddha name and the colophon appear to form a single group, in fact, the colophon refers to the sutra passage carved to its right (HDS 22).See 71, fig. 8. On the basis of this comparison, it may be possible to argue that there was also a carved sutra passage at Mount Tao.
On Mount Tao, the name of the donor is heavily weathered; consequently the reading by An Tingshan, mentioned above, cannot be confirmed. 135.

div.head: Carving Techniques
div.p: The carving technique is predominantly V-shaped. Slanting lines are chiselled from both sides of a stroke, so that they intersect and the stroke naturally takes shape.
